summary: The Gemara concludes the gold quality discussion (paz gold glistens like pearls; Rav Ashi enumerates five gold types), then opens Mishna chapter 5: on ordinary days a half-measure of incense is sacrificed, priests use the eastern ramp, today the High Priest uses the middle, and on ordinary days four wood-pyre arrangements are on the altar.

display.whats: What is 'paz' gold, and how many types of gold are there?
display.hint: Paz gold gets its name because it glistens like pearls. Rav Ashi counts five types of gold, each with distinctive properties - the classification matters because different Temple vessels required different quality gold.
display.title: Five Names for Gold: Rav Ashi's Taxonomy

learning.ahaMoment: Five types of gold, each with a name and a property - the Talmud preserves a complete taxonomy of gold quality for Temple use.
learning.memoryAnchor: Paz gold glistens like pearls - the name is descriptive of its appearance.
learning.learnerQuestion: How many distinct types of gold does the Torah name, and what does each name mean?
learning.coreTension: The Torah uses several different words for gold across its verses, and without a structured account a reader cannot tell whether these are just stylistic variation or genuinely distinct grades relevant to Temple vessels.
learning.coreMove: Rav Ashi lays out a taxonomy of five named gold types, each tied to a distinguishing quality or verse, establishing that the Torah's varied gold-language reflects real, distinct grades rather than interchangeable synonyms.

takeaway.text: Rav Ashi identifies five distinct types of gold named in the Torah, each with its own defining quality - the variation in gold-language across verses reflects genuinely distinct grades, not stylistic synonyms.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume the Torah's different gold-words are just stylistic variety for the same material, missing that Rav Ashi treats them as a structured taxonomy of five genuinely distinct grades.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'Paz' gold is named for its pearl-like glistening quality.
argumentFlow.label: Derivation: 'Paz' gold is named for its pearl-like

argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Ashi
argumentFlow.text: There are five types of gold, with the finest being paz from Parvaim.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: There are five types of gold, with

misconceptions.misconception: The Torah's different words for gold (zahav, zahav tov, paz, etc.) are just stylistic variation describing the same single material.
misconceptions.correction: Rav Ashi treats these as a structured taxonomy of five distinct gold grades, each defined by its own quality or verse - the varied language reflects real differences, not synonyms.

visualizableElements.label: five types of gold from ordinary to paz

quizSeeds.answer: Paz is fine gold named because it glistens like the luster of pearls (paz = pearl-like sheen).
quizSeeds.question: What does 'paz' mean and why is it called that?

sugyot.finalRuling: Five types of gold exist; paz is the finest, named for its pearl-like glistening quality.

display.whats: What incense measure was used on ordinary days vs. Yom Kippur?
display.hint: On ordinary days, a priest offered a peras (half-measure) of incense on the golden altar. On Yom Kippur, the High Priest used a different quantity inside the Kodesh Kodashim.
display.title: How Much Incense? Ordinary Days vs. Yom Kippur

learning.ahaMoment: Ordinary incense: a peras. Yom Kippur: a different quantity inside the most sacred space - the contrast defines what makes Yom Kippur unique.
learning.memoryAnchor: A peras of incense daily on the golden altar; a different measure inside the Holy of Holies on Yom Kippur.
learning.learnerQuestion: Does the Yom Kippur incense use the same measure (peras) as the daily incense offering, or a different amount?
learning.coreTension: The daily incense offering uses a fixed peras measure, but Yom Kippur's incense service - carried by the High Priest's own hands into the Holy of Holies - raises the question of whether the same fixed measure still applies or whether the unique rite calls for a different quantity.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara confirms the peras measure used for the ordinary daily incense also governs the Yom Kippur incense, showing that despite all the ways the Yom Kippur rite differs in vessel and procedure, the quantity measure itself remains constant.

takeaway.text: The peras measure of incense used on ordinary days also governs the Yom Kippur incense offering - the quantity stays constant even though the vessels and procedure differ.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume that because the Yom Kippur incense rite differs so much in vessel, carrier, and location, its incense quantity must differ too, missing that the Gemara holds the peras measure constant across both.

argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: On ordinary days a peras of incense is offered on the golden altar.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: On ordinary days a peras of incense

halachic: incense quantity daily vs Yom Kippur

misconceptions.misconception: Since the Yom Kippur incense service differs from the daily service in vessel, carrier, and location, it must also use a different quantity of incense.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara holds the incense quantity constant: the same peras measure used for the ordinary daily incense also governs the Yom Kippur incense, even though the vessels and procedure differ.

visualizableElements.label: ordinary day peras vs Yom Kippur quantity

quizSeeds.answer: A peras (half-measure) of incense on the golden altar.
quizSeeds.question: What quantity of incense was used on ordinary days?

sugyot.finalRuling: On ordinary days a peras of incense is offered; the Yom Kippur quantity is different and larger.

display.whats: Which ramp did priests use on ordinary days, and which did the High Priest use today?
display.hint: On ordinary days, priests ascend the altar via the eastern side of the ramp. On Yom Kippur, the High Priest ascends via the middle of the ramp - a special path reserved for this day.
display.title: Eastern Ramp for the Daily Rite, Middle Ramp for the High Priest

learning.ahaMoment: Same ramp, different path - the middle path of the kevesh is reserved for the High Priest on Yom Kippur.
learning.memoryAnchor: East side for priests on ordinary days; middle of the ramp for the High Priest on Yom Kippur.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the High Priest ascend the altar by the middle ramp on Yom Kippur instead of the eastern ramp used on ordinary days?
learning.coreTension: Ordinary priests ascend the altar's ramp from the east side for the daily service, but the Yom Kippur procedure has the High Priest use the middle of the ramp, raising the question of what about his role or the day calls for a different approach path.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara explains the middle-ramp ascent as an expression of honor for the High Priest, distinguishing his approach from the ordinary priests' eastern approach to mark his elevated status on the day's central rite.

takeaway.text: Ordinary priests ascend the altar ramp from the east for the daily service, but the High Priest ascends via the middle of the ramp on Yom Kippur - a deliberate mark of honor distinguishing his role.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume the ramp approach is identical for all priests regardless of day or rank, missing that the middle-ramp ascent is a specific honor reserved for the High Priest on Yom Kippur.

argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: Ordinary priests use the eastern side of the ramp; today the High Priest uses the middle.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Ordinary priests use the eastern side of

halachic: ramp path for High Priest on Yom Kippur

requiresUnderstanding: High Priest's special status on Yom Kippur

misconceptions.misconception: All priests, including the High Priest on Yom Kippur, ascend the altar ramp the same way, from the east side.
misconceptions.correction: The High Priest ascends via the middle of the ramp on Yom Kippur, distinct from the eastern approach used by ordinary priests in the daily service - a deliberate mark of honor for his role.

visualizableElements.label: altar ramp - east side vs middle path

quizSeeds.answer: The middle of the ramp, as opposed to ordinary priests who use the eastern side.
quizSeeds.question: Which path on the ramp did the High Priest use on Yom Kippur?

sugyot.finalRuling: On Yom Kippur the High Priest uses the middle path of the altar ramp; ordinary priests use the eastern path.

display.whats: How many wood-pyre arrangements were on the altar on ordinary days vs. Yom Kippur?
display.hint: On ordinary days there were four wood-pyre arrangements on the altar; on Yom Kippur the arrangement was different. The Mishna specifies the ordinary baseline so the Yom Kippur variation can be understood.
display.title: Four Wood Arrangements: The Baseline Pyre

learning.ahaMoment: Four wood arrangements on ordinary days; the Yom Kippur arrangement is the subject of rabbinic dispute.
learning.memoryAnchor: Four wood-pyre arrangements on ordinary days on the outer altar.
learning.learnerQuestion: How many separate wood arrangements were set on the altar, and what was each one for?
learning.coreTension: The altar fire requires organized wood arrangements rather than an undifferentiated pile, so the Gemara must establish the baseline number and purpose of these arrangements before the later dispute over whether Yom Kippur adds a fifth.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara lays out four distinct wood arrangements, each serving a specific function in the daily Temple fire system, establishing the baseline structure that the later R. Meir/R. Yehuda dispute will build on.

takeaway.text: The altar fire is organized into four distinct wood arrangements, each with its own specific function, not a single undifferentiated pile of wood.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may picture the altar fire as one generic woodpile, missing that the Gemara establishes four distinct, purpose-built arrangements as the baseline structure.

argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: On ordinary days four wood-pyre arrangements are on the altar.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: On ordinary days four wood-pyre arrangements are

misconceptions.misconception: The wood on the altar is just one undifferentiated pile, with no meaningful distinction between separate arrangements.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara establishes four distinct wood arrangements on the altar, each serving its own specific function within the daily Temple fire system.

visualizableElements.label: four wood arrangements on the altar

quizSeeds.question: How many wood-pyre arrangements were on the altar on ordinary days?

sugyot.finalRuling: Four wood-pyre arrangements are on the altar on ordinary days.

display.whats: How did the High Priest sanctify his hands and feet differently on Yom Kippur vs. ordinary days?
display.hint: On ordinary days, a priest sanctifies his hands and feet from the laver. On Yom Kippur, the High Priest sanctifies from the golden pitcher. This is a specific Yom Kippur distinction noted in the Mishna.
display.title: Laver Water for Ordinary Days, Golden Pitcher for Yom Kippur

learning.ahaMoment: Golden pitcher for Yom Kippur hand-foot sanctification vs. the ordinary laver - the vessel itself signals the day's sanctity.
learning.memoryAnchor: Ordinary day: laver for sanctification. Yom Kippur: golden pitcher.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the High Priest use a golden pitcher to sanctify his hands and feet on Yom Kippur instead of the laver used on ordinary days?
learning.coreTension: Ordinary priests sanctify their hands and feet from the laver before service, but the Yom Kippur procedure substitutes a golden pitcher for the High Priest, raising the question of why the day's heightened sanctity requires departing from the standard laver.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara explains the golden-pitcher substitution as fitting the elevated, vessel-upgraded character of the Yom Kippur service, paralleling the earlier silver-to-gold upgrade seen with the coal pan.

takeaway.text: Ordinary priests sanctify their hands and feet from the laver, but on Yom Kippur the High Priest uses a golden pitcher instead - another instance of the day's vessels being upgraded to match its heightened sanctity.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume the same laver is used for hand/foot sanctification on every day of Temple service, missing that Yom Kippur substitutes a golden pitcher specifically for the High Priest.

argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: On Yom Kippur the High Priest uses a golden pitcher for hand-foot sanctification; otherwise the laver.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: On Yom Kippur the High Priest uses

halachic: golden pitcher for Yom Kippur sanctification

misconceptions.misconception: The High Priest sanctifies his hands and feet from the same laver used by ordinary priests on every day, including Yom Kippur.
misconceptions.correction: On Yom Kippur the High Priest uses a golden pitcher instead of the laver - a vessel upgrade matching the day's heightened sanctity, similar to the silver-to-gold upgrade seen with the coal pan.

visualizableElements.label: golden pitcher vs laver for hand sanctification

quizSeeds.answer: A golden pitcher, as opposed to the ordinary copper laver.
quizSeeds.question: What vessel was used for hand-foot sanctification on Yom Kippur?

sugyot.finalRuling: On Yom Kippur a golden pitcher is used for the High Priest's hand-foot sanctification instead of the ordinary laver.

display.whats: How many wood-pyre arrangements were on the altar, and what was the dispute between R. Meir and R. Yehuda?
display.hint: The Mishna says four arrangements; R. Meir and R. Yehuda dispute whether there is a fifth arrangement needed for the Yom Kippur avodah. The Gemara analyzes each arrangement's source and function.
display.title: Five Arrangements or Four? R. Meir and R. Yehuda Dispute the Pyre

learning.ahaMoment: R. Meir: five arrangements (one more for Yom Kippur coals). R. Yehuda: four suffice. The Gemara derives each arrangement from a separate Torah verse.
learning.memoryAnchor: Four vs. five wood arrangements - the extra one for Yom Kippur is the dispute. Each arrangement comes from a different Torah verse.
learning.learnerQuestion: Does Yom Kippur require a fifth wood arrangement beyond the four used on ordinary days?
learning.coreTension: R. Meir holds that Yom Kippur requires an extra wood arrangement specifically for the incense coals beyond the baseline four, while R. Yehuda holds the same four arrangements suffice even on Yom Kippur, and each grounds his position in a verse-by-verse reading.
learning.coreMove: R. Meir derives a fifth arrangement from the verse's wording to supply coals for the Yom Kippur incense specifically, while R. Yehuda reads the same verses as not requiring any addition beyond the standard four - the dispute turns on competing derivations from the same textual material.

takeaway.text: R. Meir holds Yom Kippur requires a fifth wood arrangement to supply coals for the incense, while R. Yehuda holds the standard four arrangements suffice even on Yom Kippur - both positions are derived from close readings of the same verses.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume the number of wood arrangements is a settled, undisputed fact, missing that R. Meir and R. Yehuda actively dispute whether Yom Kippur adds a fifth arrangement, each from verse-based derivation.

argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: Four wood-pyre arrangements on the altar on ordinary days.
argumentFlow.label: Mishna: Four wood-pyre arrangements on the altar on

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: First arrangement: main fire for offerings, from 'fire on the altar.'
argumentFlow.label: Derivation: First arrangement: main fire for offerings, from

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Second arrangement: incense fire from 'he shall burn incense in the morning.'
argumentFlow.label: Derivation: Second arrangement: incense fire from 'he shall

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Third and fourth arrangements: additional wood for the main fire.
argumentFlow.label: Derivation: Third and fourth arrangements: additional wood for

argumentFlow.speaker: R. Meir/R. Yehuda
argumentFlow.text: R. Meir: five arrangements; R. Yehuda: four. The fifth would be for Yom Kippur coals.
argumentFlow.label: Dispute: R. Meir: five arrangements; R. Yehuda: four.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Resolution: R. Yehuda derives the Yom Kippur coal source from one of the four; R. Meir needs a separate fifth.
argumentFlow.label: Resolution: Resolution: R. Yehuda derives the Yom Kippur

misconceptions.misconception: The number of wood arrangements on the altar is a fixed, undisputed fact rather than a matter of tannaitic disagreement.
misconceptions.correction: R. Meir and R. Yehuda dispute the count: R. Meir derives a fifth arrangement for the Yom Kippur incense coals, while R. Yehuda holds four suffice even on Yom Kippur - each position rests on a verse-based derivation.

visualizableElements.label: R. Meir 5 arrangements vs R. Yehuda 4
four arrangements derived from four Torah verses

quizSeeds.answer: He required a fifth arrangement specifically to provide the coals for the Yom Kippur incense firepan, which R. Yehuda derived from one of the four standard arrangements.
quizSeeds.question: Why did R. Meir hold there were five wood arrangements on the altar?

sugyot.finalRuling: On ordinary days four wood-pyre arrangements; R. Meir requires a fifth for Yom Kippur coal sourcing.

glossary.en: Half-measure (half a maneh) of incense offered on the golden altar in the Sanctuary on ordinary days.
The ramp leading up to the altar; priests used the east side normally, the High Priest used the middle on Yom Kippur.
Finest grade gold, named because it glistens like the luster of pearls; used for the most elevated Temple vessels.

rashiTranslations.en: The glistening of paz gold resembles the luster of pearls - it has a particular sheen.
Rav Ashi: there are five types of gold, paz being the finest from Parvaim.
On ordinary days, a peras (half-measure) of incense was offered in the morning and evening.
Priests ascend the eastern side of the altar ramp on ordinary days.
Today (Yom Kippur) the High Priest uses the middle path of the ramp.
On every other day there were four wood-pyre arrangements on the outer altar.
The Gemara asks: how can only four arrangements be there? The Mishna seems to imply more.
The arrangements are derived from multiple verses about the fire on the altar.
First arrangement: the main fire for burning the offerings.
Second arrangement: the fire from which coals are taken for the incense altar inside.
Third arrangement: continuous wood added to maintain the main fire.
Fourth arrangement: additional wood on the corners of the altar.
R. Meir holds a fifth arrangement was needed for the Yom Kippur coal-scooping.
R. Yehuda: the Yom Kippur coals come from the same fire as the daily incense coals.
The dispute: whether a separate fire was designated for Yom Kippur incense coals.
The Gemara derives each arrangement from a separate verse in the Torah.
'A perpetual fire shall burn on the altar' - this establishes the main fire requirement.
'The fire on the altar shall be kept burning' - a second mention establishes a second arrangement.
The Gemara reconciles how R. Yehuda needs only four while R. Meir needs five.
Each wood arrangement had priests assigned to maintain it throughout the day.
The wood used had to be dry and free of worms - requirements for Temple use.
Specific tree species were permitted for the altar fire: fig, nut, pine, and others.
The Jerusalem Talmud records a tradition about which families supplied wood.
Wood festival (Chag Ha'Etzim) - days when families brought wood to the Temple.
The Mishna in Taanit records the family wood-donation days.
The altar fire had to burn continuously - it could never be extinguished.
If the fire went out, priests had to relight it - but they could not use foreign fire.
Only fire from the main altar arrangement was permitted for relighting.
The coals for the Yom Kippur firepan had to come from the altar fire specifically.
The dispute between R. Meir and R. Yehuda about five vs. four arrangements is unresolved in the Mishna.
Practical implication: how the High Priest knew which fire to scoop from.
The arrangements were in specific physical locations on the altar.
Corner arrangement vs. center arrangement had different physical dimensions.
The Gemara will now proceed to discuss the wood-pyre laws of ordinary days more fully.
45a ends with the R. Meir/R. Yehuda dispute about the number of wood arrangements unresolved.
The next page will resolve this through additional verse analysis.
The five types of gold mentioned by Rav Ashi at the start of 45a are: zahav, zahav tov, paz, ketam, and charuz.
Each gold type has a specific quality marker: color, origin, purity level, or glistening property.
The Temple menorah was made of zahav tahor (pure gold); the Ark overlaid with zahav tahor as well.
The distinction between gold types was practically important when purchasing gold for Temple repairs.
Paz from Parvaim is the finest and most precious; ketam is the most orange-toned.
Charuz gold is named for its thread-like drawing quality - it can be drawn into threads.
These distinctions were known to the craftsmen who made Temple vessels.
The Gemara preserves this taxonomy as part of its broader interest in Temple material culture.
